commentary: After years on the line, I'm finally putting plans on paper for a restaurant of my own. Rootstock will be a 40-seat seasonal-American spot built on Hudson Valley sourcing. Long road ahead, but it feels right. More to come.

Proud of the team at Harborview Kitchen tonight. We ran a duck special with a Jersey peach gastrique and the room loved it. Seasonal cooking only works when the whole line buys in. Grateful for this crew.
Spent the morning walking rows at a Hudson Valley farm with the grower. If you cook for a living and you've never met the person who grows your food, change that. It will change how you cook.
Five years ago I was a line cook who couldn't get a station. Tonight I expedited a full Saturday service as sous chef. Growth in this industry is slow and unglamorous and absolutely worth it.
Touring a 40-seat space in Hoboken this week for Rootstock. Brick, big windows, a kitchen I could actually work in. Still early, but it's the first one that felt like it could be home.

Reminder for anyone building toward their own place: save more than the business plan says you need. The gap between projection and reality is where most first-timers get caught. Saving steadily, one service at a time.

Local sourcing isn't a marketing line for me. It's Greenfield's heirlooms, Fontana's cheese, day-boat fish from Fulton. Real relationships with real producers. That's the foundation Rootstock is built on.
Staff meal is the most important plate we cook all day. Feed your people well and they'll feed your guests better. Tonight: braised short rib over polenta for the crew that earns it.
Mentorship matters in this business. Grateful for the retired restaurateurs who answer my buildout questions at odd hours and tell me the truth even when it's not what I want to hear.
Testing a corn, brown butter, and lime plate for the Rootstock opening menu. Simple ingredients, but it has to be perfect. This is the part of the work I love most.
Food cost is where good restaurants quietly die. Spent my day off building a costing spreadsheet for every dish I want to open with. Romantic? No. Necessary? Completely.
To the young cooks grinding through prep shifts and double services: the work compounds. The knife skills, the palate, the calm under pressure. Keep showing up. It adds up faster than you think.
Coffee program update: Rootstock will do real pour-over, single-origin from a local roaster. Small detail, but the details are the whole job. Harborside Roasters has me obsessed.
Walked my first commercial lease past a mentor this week. The legalese is humbling. If you're opening, do not sign anything without someone who's been burned reading it first.
Twelve years ago I earned my Culinary Arts diploma at Hudson Valley Culinary Academy with no idea where it would lead. Tonight I'm a sous chef sketching my own restaurant. Grateful for every step.
Hiring philosophy for Rootstock, still a ways off but I think about it constantly: hire for care and curiosity, train the skill. A cook who gives a damn beats a resume every time.
Seasonal menus mean throwing out dishes you love because the ingredient's gone. Said goodbye to a tomato course this week. That discipline is the whole point. Let the season lead.

Year in review: more responsibility at Harborview, real progress on the Rootstock fund, and a clearer picture of the restaurant I want to build. Slow and steady. Thank you to everyone walking this with me.
